title: Fine-Tuning vs. Prompt Engineering: When to Use What
author: AI Systems Engineering Team
excerpt: The eternal question: should you fine-tune a model or just write better prompts? The answer depends on your use case, budget, and timeline.

content.text: You're building an AI feature. The LLM doesn't quite get your domain — maybe it's a legal assistant that needs to cite specific case law, or a customer support bot that must follow your company's exact troubleshooting protocol.
You have two options: (1) Spend weeks collecting data and fine-tuning a model, or (2) Spend a day writing detailed prompts with few-shot examples.
Most teams default to fine-tuning because it feels like 'real engineering.' But often, prompt engineering is faster, cheaper, and more maintainable.

items: The task is well-defined and can be described in instructions
You need to ship in days, not weeks
Your use case changes frequently (prompts are easier to update than models)
You don't have thousands of labeled examples
You want to compare multiple models quickly
The task requires deep domain knowledge that can't fit in a prompt (e.g., medical diagnosis)
Latency is critical — fine-tuned models can be smaller and faster
You need consistent output format that the base model struggles with
You want to reduce API costs by using a smaller model

content.text: The best teams use both. They start with prompt engineering to validate the use case, then gradually introduce fine-tuning for the parts that matter most.
A practical workflow: (1) Build with prompts, (2) Collect real user interactions, (3) Use those interactions to create a fine-tuning dataset, (4) Fine-tune when the economics justify it.

Fine-tuning GPT-4 costs ~$30-80 per million tokens of training data. Running inference on a fine-tuned GPT-3.5 costs the same as base GPT-3.5. But a well-optimized prompt on GPT-4 might solve the problem with zero additional training cost.
For most business applications, start with prompts. Move to fine-tuning only when you have evidence that prompts can't get you there.
